name: Tirzepatide
summary: Tirzepatide is a once-weekly dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist used clinically for type 2 diabetes (Mounjaro) and chronic weight management (Zepbound). In phase 3 trials it produced large reductions in body weight and HbA1c. It is FDA-approved for those indications; use for other purposes is off-label or investigational.
mechanism:
Tirzepatide is a single synthetic 39-amino-acid peptide engineered from the GIP sequence with a C20 fatty-diacid moiety that promotes albumin binding and extends its half-life. It simultaneously activates two incretin hormone receptors: glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1). GLP-1 receptor agonism enhances glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon, slows gastric emptying, and reduces appetite through central pathways; GIP receptor agonism adds further insulinotropic effect and is thought to act synergistically with GLP-1 signaling on energy balance and adipose handling.

The net effect is improved glycemic control, reduced food intake, and substantial weight loss. Unlike triple agonists such as retatrutide, tirzepatide has no glucagon-receptor (GCGR) activity, so its weight effect derives from the combined incretin actions rather than from increased energy expenditure via glucagon.
benefits: Mean weight loss up to ~20.9% at 72 weeks (15 mg) in SURMOUNT-1 (obesity without diabetes)
Large HbA1c reductions in type 2 diabetes (~2.0-2.4% lowering in SURPASS trials)
Clinically meaningful weight loss (~14.7% at 15 mg) in people with type 2 diabetes (SURMOUNT-2)
Reduced appetite and food intake via delayed gastric emptying and central satiety signaling
Improvements in blood pressure, lipids, and markers of hepatic steatosis (MASLD)
risks: Boxed warning for thyroid C-cell tumors (medullary thyroid carcinoma) based on rodent data; class effect of incretin agonists
Risk of hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas)
Possible acute pancreatitis and gallbladder disease (cholelithiasis, cholecystitis)
Delayed gastric emptying may reduce absorption of oral medications and reduce efficacy of oral contraceptives
Rapid weight loss can be associated with loss of lean mass if protein intake/resistance training are inadequate
FDA-approved only for type 2 diabetes and chronic weight management; other uses are off-label. Compounded/gray-market product quality is not guaranteed
sideEffects: Nausea (most common, especially during dose escalation)

dosage.notes: Common educational protocol mirrors the labeled titration: start at 2.5 mg once weekly for 4 weeks (a non-therapeutic starting dose intended to reduce GI side effects), then increase by 2.5 mg every 4 weeks as tolerated (2.5 -> 5 -> 7.5 -> 10 -> 12.5 -> 15 mg). Maximum studied dose is 15 mg weekly. Slower titration reduces nausea.

cycleLength: Continuous long-term use in trials (52-72 weeks); no defined off-cycle. Steady state reached after ~4 weeks. Weight regain is common after discontinuation.

reconstitution.notes: Branded Mounjaro/Zepbound pens/vials come premixed and require no reconstitution. For lyophilized research vials, adding 1 mL bacteriostatic water to a 10 mg vial yields 10 mg/mL (so 0.25 mL = 2.5 mg); 2 mL into 10 mg gives 5 mg/mL for finer dosing. Swirl gently, do not shake.

storage: Branded product: refrigerate at 2-8 C; pens may be kept at room temperature (below 30 C) for limited periods per label. Lyophilized research vials: store frozen/cool (-20 C long-term, or 2-8 C); reconstituted solution refrigerate at 2-8 C and use within ~4 weeks. Protect from light; do not freeze reconstituted solution.
contraindications: Personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma
Known hypersensitivity to tirzepatide or excipients

interactions.note: Increased risk of hypoglycemia; insulin dose may need reduction with close glucose monitoring.
Increased hypoglycemia risk when combined; secretagogue dose may need reduction.
Both are overlapping incretin (GLP-1/GIP) agonists; stacking compounds GI side effects, hypoglycemia, and gastric-emptying delay without added benefit. Do not combine.
Overlapping GLP-1 receptor agonism; combining two incretin agonists compounds nausea and GI risk without added benefit. Do not stack.
GHRH analogs can worsen glucose tolerance while tirzepatide improves it; monitor glycemia closely if combined.

Sometimes combined anecdotally for GI discomfort during incretin titration, but there is no controlled human data supporting any benefit or safety of the combination. Educational note only — not a recommendation to stack.

references.title: Jastreboff AM et al. Tirzepatide Once Weekly for the Treatment of Obesity (SURMOUNT-1). NEJM 2022
Garvey WT et al. Tirzepatide once weekly for the treatment of obesity in people with type 2 diabetes (SURMOUNT-2). The Lancet 2023
Frias JP et al. Tirzepatide versus Semaglutide Once Weekly in Type 2 Diabetes (SURPASS-2). NEJM 2021
Farzam K, Patel P. Tirzepatide. StatPearls (NCBI Bookshelf), 2024
Schneck KB et al. Population pharmacokinetics of the GIP/GLP receptor agonist tirzepatide. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol 2024 (PMC)
